title: Dynamic dispatch in action
instruction:
When you call a method through a parent-type reference, Java automatically runs the child's overridden version at runtime. This is **dynamic dispatch** — the most powerful feature of OOP.

Run this — even though the variable type is `Animal`, the right sound plays.
successMessage: The array holds Animal references — but at runtime Java knows each object's real type and calls the right makeSound(). This is dynamic dispatch — it's what makes polymorphism work.

title: Upcasting and downcasting
instruction:
**Upcasting**: assigning a child type to a parent reference — always safe.
**Downcasting**: assigning a parent reference back to a child — requires explicit cast, can fail.

Always use `instanceof` before downcasting.
successMessage: Upcasting is automatic. Downcasting needs instanceof check — without it, you get ClassCastException at runtime. Pattern matching (instanceof Car car) checks and casts in one shot.

title: Polymorphic collections
instruction:
Store different types in a single collection using the parent type. Process all of them uniformly — each handles itself.

This is the Open/Closed Principle: open for extension (add new shape types), closed for modification (existing code unchanged).
successMessage: One loop processes three different types. Add a Hexagon class later? Just extend Shape and add to the list — zero changes to existing code. That's the Open/Closed principle.

title: Overriding equals and hashCode
instruction:
By default, `equals()` compares references (same memory). Override it to compare content.

**Rule**: if you override `equals()`, you MUST override `hashCode()` too — they're used together in `HashMap` and `HashSet`.
successMessage: Overriding both equals AND hashCode: a and b are 'equal' so the HashSet treats them as duplicates — size is 2, not 3. Always override both together — IDEs can generate them automatically.

title: Design a notification system
instruction:
Final challenge — no starter code.

Design:
- `abstract class Notification` with fields `String title`, `String message` and abstract `send()` method
- `class EmailNotification` — send() prints `"Email to [email]: [title] — [message]"`
- `class SMSNotification` — send() prints `"SMS to [phone]: [message]"`
- `class PushNotification` — send() prints `"Push: [title]"`

Write `sendAll(List<Notification> notifications)` and test it with all three types.
hint: import java.util.List; abstract class Notification { ... } class EmailNotification extends Notification { ... }

codeChecks.message: Write sendAll with a List<Notification> parameter.

successMessage: A polymorphic notification system — sendAll doesn't care which notification type it receives. Add SlackNotification later? Zero changes to sendAll. Chapter 13 done!
